lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Alack, ’tis he: why, he was met even now

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: As mad as the vex’d sea; singing aloud;

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Crown’d with rank fumiter and furrow-weeds,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: With bur-docks, hemlock, nettles, cuckoo-flowers,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Darnel, and all the idle weeds that grow

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: In our sustaining corn. A century send forth;

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Search every acre in the high-grown field,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: And bring him to our eye.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: What can man’s wisdom

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: In the restoring his bereaved sense?

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: He that helps him take all my outward worth.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: There is means, madam:

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Our foster-nurse of nature is repose,

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: The which he lacks; that to provoke in him,

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Are many simples operative, whose power

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Will close the eye of anguish.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: All blest secrets,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: All you unpublish’d virtues of the earth,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Spring with my tears! be aidant and remediate

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: In the good man’s distress! Seek, seek for him;

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Lest his ungovern’d rage dissolve the life

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: That wants the means to lead it.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: News, madam;

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: The British powers are marching hitherward.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: ’Tis known before; our preparation stands

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: In expectation of them. O dear father,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: It is thy business that I go about;

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Therefore great France

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: My mourning and important tears hath pitied.

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: No blown ambition doth our arms incite,

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: But love, dear love, and our aged father’s right:

lines.speaker: CORDELIA
lines.text: Soon may I hear and see him!

stage_directions.text: Enter, with drum and colours, Cordelia, Doctor, and Soldiers.

glosses.definition: poisonous herb — traditional symbol of death
lady's smock; fool's parsley — flowers associated with cuckoldry
worthless plants — the crown of weeds Lear now wears in his madness
Cordelia's pledge — she fights for Lear's restoration, not conquest
luxuriant fumitory — a weed in the wreath on Lear's head
external greatness — Cordelia's army is not for gain but for Lear

annotations.title: Cordelia entering crowned with weeds — the plant-catalogue of Lear's madness
annotations.body: Cordelia enters describing Lear 'As mad as the vex'd sea; singing aloud; / Crown'd with rank fumiter and furrow-weeds, / With hardoks, hemlock, nettles, cuckoo-flowers, / Darnel, and all the idle weeds that grow / In our sustaining corn.' The catalogue is both botanical and symbolic: fumitory (smoke / self-deception), hemlock (poison / Socratic death), nettles (burning), cuckoo-flowers (cuckoldry), darnel (drunkenness in folk medicine). Every plant in the crown has a moral valence. Shakespeare is signaling that Lear's madness is legible — you can read his crown of weeds. The line-count is small but the image is among the play's densest. Pre-Raphaelite painters (Ford Madox Brown, for instance) returned to it repeatedly.
sources: Jonathan Bate, Soul of the Age (2008)

trials.prompt: What is symbolic about the weeds that crown Lear in his madness?
options: Each is legible — fumitory (self-deception), hemlock (poison/Socratic death), nettles (burning), cuckoo-flowers (cuckoldry), darnel (drunkenness) — his crown is a readable moral emblem
They identify the time of year
